paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 23: The Friday Soup (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 4 girl who volunteers every Friday evening at a community kitchen in Bedok for her Community Involvement Programme hours, and her unexpected exchange with a quiet eight-year-old boy from Yangon. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
I had started for the certificate. That was the truth of it, and I did not pretend otherwise. The Sec 4 CIP hours had to be done, and a friend from church had told me about the community kitchen at the void deck of Block 124 in Bedok, where families could come for a hot meal on Friday evenings. I signed up for eight weeks. Eight weeks would be enough.

Paragraph 2
The kitchen ran out of two folding tables and a borrowed soup pot the size of a small drum. Auntie Faridah ladled rice. Uncle Raj stirred the curry. My job, the easiest one, was the soup — fishball soup, every Friday, the same recipe. I stood behind a tray of plastic bowls and waited for the queue to form at six o'clock.

Paragraph 3
The boy came on my second Friday. He was perhaps eight years old, thin in a way that made his school uniform look borrowed. His mother held his hand. She spoke softly to Auntie Faridah in an accent I later learned was Burmese. They had come from Yangon a year ago, Auntie Faridah told me afterwards, and the boy was in Primary 2 at the school nearby. His name was Wai Yan.

Paragraph 4
He did not speak. Not that first Friday, not the second, not the third. He held out his bowl with both hands, and I filled it, and he nodded once — a small, precise nod — and walked back to the table where his mother sat. I told myself it was shyness. I told myself it was the language. I did not, at first, think very much about it at all.

Paragraph 5
For two months, this was our arrangement. Six o'clock, the queue, the bowl, the nod. I learned the weight of the ladle in my wrist. I learned which fishballs sank and which floated. I learned, without realising I was learning it, the shape of Wai Yan's small hands as he reached up for his bowl, and the careful way he carried it back to his mother without spilling a single drop.

Paragraph 6
The Friday before the school holidays was the last one I had signed up for. The queue was longer than usual. The air at the void deck was thick with the smell of curry and steamed rice and the sweet damp of a coming rain. When Wai Yan reached the front of the line, I lifted the ladle as I had done seven times before, and poured the soup carefully into his bowl.

Paragraph 7
He did not walk away. He stood there, holding the bowl, and looked up at me. Then, slowly, carefully, as though each syllable had been rehearsed in front of a mirror, he said: 'Xiè xie, jie jie.' Thank you, big sister. In Mandarin. A language that was neither his nor entirely mine. I was so unprepared that I simply stared. He nodded once — that same small, precise nod — and walked back to his table.

Paragraph 8
I walked home that night through the drizzle without an umbrella. I had thought, all along, that I was the one giving. The certificate would say so. The hours would be counted. But somewhere between the second Friday and the eighth, an eight-year-old boy from Yangon had been quietly practising two words of Mandarin for a stranger at a soup pot, and I had not noticed. What I had thought I was giving, I realised, was less than what I had been receiving, week after week, without seeing it at all.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. CIP: Community Involvement Programme, compulsory community service hours for Singapore secondary students; void deck: the open ground floor of an HDB block, often used for community functions; jie jie: Mandarin for 'older sister'.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what does the narrator admit was her original reason for volunteering at the community kitchen? [1]
questions.answer: she had started purely for the certificate / to clock her compulsory Sec 4 CIP hours, not out of genuine concern for the families

questions.stem: Q2(a). In Paragraph 2, why does the narrator describe her job as 'the easiest one'? [1]
questions.answer: all she had to do was ladle fishball soup from the same recipe each Friday, while Auntie Faridah and Uncle Raj handled the rice and curry

questions.stem: Q2(b). What is suggested by the comparison of the soup pot to 'a small drum' (Paragraph 2)? [1]
questions.answer: the pot is unusually large, hinting at the size of the operation and the number of families the kitchen was feeding each Friday

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 3, what does the phrase 'thin in a way that made his school uniform look borrowed' suggest about Wai Yan? [1]
questions.answer: he is so thin that his uniform appears too big for him, hinting that his family is struggling and possibly that he is undernourished

questions.stem: Q4. In Paragraph 4, what TWO explanations did the narrator give herself for Wai Yan's silence? [1]
questions.answer: she told herself it was shyness and that it was because of the language barrier

questions.stem: Q5(a). What does the word 'arrangement' in Paragraph 5 suggest about the weekly exchange between the narrator and Wai Yan? [1]
questions.answer: it had become a fixed, unspoken routine between them — a quiet pattern repeated without words being needed

questions.stem: Q5(b). Explain the effect of the phrase 'without realising I was learning it' (Paragraph 5). [1]
questions.answer: it shows that the narrator was absorbing small details about Wai Yan unconsciously, suggesting that she was paying more attention to him than she herself knew at the time

questions.stem: Q6. In Paragraph 7, why does the narrator describe Wai Yan as speaking 'as though each syllable had been rehearsed in front of a mirror'? [1]
questions.answer: it suggests that Wai Yan had deliberately and carefully practised the Mandarin phrase ahead of time, making the moment of thanks far more meaningful than a casual remark

questions.stem: Q7. What is striking about the narrator's description of Mandarin as 'a language that was neither his nor entirely mine' (Paragraph 7)? [1]
questions.answer: Wai Yan chose to thank her in a language that was not his mother tongue, and that the narrator herself does not fully own — the effort of meeting halfway in a borrowed language makes the gesture more touching

questions.stem: Q8. In Paragraph 8, what is the effect of the contrast between 'The certificate would say so' and 'an eight-year-old boy from Yangon had been quietly practising two words of Mandarin for a stranger at a soup pot'? [1]
questions.answer: the certificate measures her giving only on paper, but the boy's quiet preparation shows a much deeper kind of giving that no certificate can record — the contrast exposes how shallow her original motive was

questions.stem: Q9. Explain in your own words what the narrator means when she says 'What I had thought I was giving, I realised, was less than what I had been receiving' (Paragraph 8). [1]
questions.answer: she had assumed she was the one helping the families, but in fact Wai Yan's silent kindness and his rehearsed words of thanks had been a far greater gift to her than the bowl of soup she handed him each week

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of the small detail that the narrator walked home 'through the drizzle without an umbrella' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: it suggests she was too absorbed in what had just happened to notice the rain, and that the discomfort of being wet seemed small compared to the realisation she was sitting with

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling about her Friday evenings at the community kitchen changes as the eight weeks unfold. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: routine / curious / impatient / proud / moved / changed / bored / dismissive. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Friday Soup' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift over the eight Fridays — from a businesslike, routine approach in the early weeks at the soup pot, to a small flicker of curiosity about the silent boy, to being deeply moved when he finally speaks, ending in a quiet but real change in how she sees what she has been doing. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 2 as she stands behind the tray of plastic bowls waiting for the queue to form at six o'clock? [1]

Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 3–4 as she begins to notice Wai Yan's silence and learns from Auntie Faridah that his family arrived from Yangon a year ago? [1]
Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7 when Wai Yan looks up at her and slowly, carefully says 'Xiè xie, jie jie'? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as she walks home through the drizzle, realising that what she had thought she was giving was less than what she had been receiving? [1]
